text: As so many decisions are made on the basis of what is scientifically known, it is important that the public has trust in scientists. During a time of crisis, this trust can be a powerful tool in influencing decisions that are made based on the available scientific research. However, the epistemic authority of science has been challenged in recent years, with resulting public distrust of scientists an area of concern. Using a pre-registered survey of 71,922 participants from 68 countries, we investigate whether or not the public trust scientists and agree with a more social role for scientists that includes greater involvement in societal and political decision-making. Our results show, first, that in most countries, most people do indeed trust scientists and agree with the increased social role that we propose. We identify factors at the individual and country level that explain between- and within-country differences, including political ideology. While there is no evidence of a widespread distrust of scientists, our findings therefore do not dismiss the concern that a small minority of people in any given country may not trust scientists — and that these people might matter when it comes to policymakers taking the views of the public into consideration in their decisions about the relative weight to assign to scientific evidence. Our results are important for both scientists and policymakers.
